content.text: Incident Action Backlog Governance

Postmortem action items accumulate faster than they are closed when an engineering team runs at full feature capacity. Without active governance, the backlog grows into an invisible debt: every open item represents a known systemic risk that has not been mitigated. Incident action backlog governance is the set of practices that keep this debt visible, prioritised, and bounded.

The goal is not to close all items immediately — that would require unlimited engineering capacity. The goal is to ensure that the highest-risk items are closed first, that the team's reliability investment per sprint is explicit and defensible, and that items are never forgotten simply because they are old.

attrs.alt: Incident action backlog triage funnel: all new items flow through weekly EM priority triage, then through a capacity gate into the sprint (20% reliability budget per sprint), then through verification to close.

content.text: The Reliability Capacity Budget

A recurring complaint in engineering organisations is that postmortem action items never get done because the sprint is always full. The reliability capacity budget addresses this structurally by reserving a fixed percentage of sprint capacity for reliability work — a floor, not a ceiling.
Team Incident History
Recommended Reliability Budget
Rationale
0–1 P0/P1 incidents in past 90 days
15% of sprint capacity
Low incident rate; maintenance mode
2–3 P0/P1 incidents in past 90 days
20% of sprint capacity
Normal operational health target
4+ P0/P1 incidents in past 90 days
30% of sprint capacity
Elevated incident rate; reliability investment required
Repeat failure rate > 20%
35% of sprint capacity, reviewed by Director
Systemic issues not being closed; escalated reliability mode

The reliability budget covers all reliability work, not just postmortem action items — monitoring improvements, load testing, runbook reviews, and infrastructure hardening all count. Postmortem action items compete within the budget with other reliability investments rather than outside normal sprint planning.

Reliability budget is a floor, not a ceiling. If the team has no action items and no reliability debt, they may use the budget for other work. But the next sprint's budget is not carried forward — use it or lose it.
Priority Scoring
Not all action items are equal. An Engineering Manager running a weekly triage uses three dimensions to prioritise the backlog.
Priority Dimensions
Dimension
Score 3 (High)
Score 2 (Medium)
Score 1 (Low)
Recurrence risk
Same failure mode caused > 1 prior incident
Plausible recurrence in similar conditions
Unlikely to recur without triggering condition
Blast radius if unmitigated
P0 or P1 if the failure recurs
P2 or P3 if the failure recurs
Near-miss or cosmetic impact
Ease of implementation
< 1 day, no deployment required
1–3 days, standard deployment
> 3 days, complex change or approval needed

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ActionItemPriority:
    recurrence_risk: int     # 1-3
    blast_radius: int        # 1-3
    ease: int                # 1-3

    def score(self) -> int:
        # Weighted: recurrence + blast radius matter more than ease
        return (self.recurrence_risk * 3) + (self.blast_radius * 3) + (self.ease * 1)

    def priority_band(self) -> str:
        s = self.score()
        if s >= 15: return "P1 — address this sprint"
        if s >= 10: return "P2 — address within 2 sprints"
        if s >= 6:  return "P3 — address within quarter"
        return "P4 — backlog, review at quarter end"

# Example: known repeat failure mode, P0 blast radius, easy fix
p = ActionItemPriority(recurrence_risk=3, blast_radius=3, ease=3)
print(p.priority_band())  # P1 — address this sprint  (score: 21)

# Low recurrence, P2 blast radius, complex change
p2 = ActionItemPriority(recurrence_risk=1, blast_radius=2, ease=1)
print(p2.priority_band())  # P3 — address within quarter  (score: 8)
Weekly Backlog Triage
The Engineering Manager runs a 30-minute weekly triage of the incident action backlog. The output is a prioritised list of items to enter the next sprint.
Triage Steps
Review all items in OPEN or OVERDUE state. How many are there? Are any from incidents more than 60 days ago?
Score each OPEN item that has not been scored yet using the three-dimension priority model
Identify P1-band items: these must enter the next sprint regardless of feature work pressure
Fill remaining reliability budget capacity with P2-band items, highest score first
Items not entering the sprint this cycle: confirm they remain assigned and not forgotten
For OVERDUE items: confirm owner has provided an updated target date; if not, initiate escalation
Backlog Age Governance
Items should not languish in the backlog indefinitely. Backlog age governance defines what happens to items that have not been addressed within a defined window.
Item Age
State
Required Action
0–30 days
OPEN or IN_PROGRESS
Normal; within SLA window for most categories
31–60 days
OPEN (not yet started)
EM triage required; must be prioritised or formally deferred
61–90 days
OPEN
Formal risk acceptance: EM documents why the item is not being closed and what compensating control exists
90+ days
OPEN
Director review: either the item is resourced and closed, or formally accepted as residual risk with an agreed review date
Any age
BLOCKED
Blocker must be resolved or item reassigned; BLOCKED items are reviewed weekly
Formal Risk Acceptance

When a postmortem action item cannot be closed within the normal SLA due to resource constraints, competing priorities, or a genuine architectural dependency, the team can formally accept the residual risk. Risk acceptance is not abandonment — it is a documented decision with a review date.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class ResidualRiskAcceptance:
    action_item_id: str
    accepted_by: str          # must be EM or above
    acceptance_date: date
    risk_description: str     # what could go wrong if this is not fixed
    compensating_control: str # what is already mitigating the risk
    review_date: date         # must be <= 90 days from acceptance
    director_approved: bool   # required if item is 90+ days old

    def is_expired(self, as_of: date | None = None) -> bool:
        check = as_of or date.today()
        return check > self.review_date

    def validate(self) -> list[str]:
        errors = []
        if not self.compensating_control:
            errors.append("Compensating control is required for risk acceptance")
        if (self.review_date - self.acceptance_date).days > 90:
            errors.append("Review date must be within 90 days of acceptance")
        return errors
Backlog Health Metrics

The incident action backlog is a lagging indicator of the organisation's reliability investment. Backlog metrics are reviewed monthly by Engineering Managers and quarterly by the Director of Engineering.
Metric
Definition
Target
Warning
Backlog size
Total open action items across all postmortems
< 2x average monthly item creation rate
Growing quarter over quarter
Mean item age (open)
Average days an item has been in OPEN state
< 21 days
> 45 days
P1-band overdue rate
% of P1-priority items that are overdue
Risk acceptance rate
% of items closed via formal risk acceptance (not implemented)
Stale items (90+ days)
Count of items older than 90 days still open
Any

from datetime import date

def compute_backlog_health(
    open_items: list,
    created_last_30_days: int,
    as_of: date | None = None,
) -> dict:
    check = as_of or date.today()
    ages = [(check - ai.created_at.date()).days for ai in open_items]
    p1_items = [ai for ai in open_items if get_priority_band(ai) == "P1"]
    p1_overdue = [ai for ai in p1_items if ai.is_overdue(check)]
    stale = [ai for ai in open_items if (check - ai.created_at.date()).days > 90]
    mean_age = sum(ages) / len(ages) if ages else 0
    return {
        "backlog_size": len(open_items),
        "mean_item_age_days": round(mean_age, 1),
        "p1_overdue_count": len(p1_overdue),
        "p1_overdue_pct": len(p1_overdue) / len(p1_items) if p1_items else 0,
        "stale_item_count": len(stale),
        "backlog_growth_rate": len(open_items) / created_last_30_days if created_last_30_days else None,
        "health": "warning" if len(stale) > 0 or len(p1_overdue) > 0 else "healthy",
    }
Integration with Sprint Planning

Incident action items are not tracked in a separate reliability tool from feature work — they live in the same project management system (GitHub Issues, Jira, Linear, or equivalent) so they compete directly and visibly for sprint capacity. A reliability item on the sprint board is harder to defer than one in a separate 'reliability Notion page nobody looks at'.
Sprint Board Configuration
Label all postmortem action items with 'incident-action' and the postmortem ID
Tag by category: 'reliability-detection', 'reliability-prevention', 'reliability-runbook', 'reliability-process'
Use severity labels that match the priority band: 'reliability-P1' through 'reliability-P4'
Set the target date as the issue due date — most project tools can alert when due dates are approaching
Link each action item issue to the postmortem document in the description
Engineering Manager Sprint Review Checklist
Count open P1-band action items — are they all in this sprint or the next one?
Count items older than 30 days — are they scored and either in sprint or formally deferred?
Verify reliability budget allocation is at or above the team's minimum threshold
Review any items that moved to BLOCKED this week — are the blockers documented and being resolved?
Update the weekly digest with any new overdue items
Backlog Amnesty

Organisations that have allowed their postmortem action backlog to grow unchecked sometimes face a situation where hundreds of items are open, many from incidents 12–24 months ago. A backlog amnesty process allows the team to formally close or accept old items and reset to a manageable state.
Pull all items older than 12 months. For each: has the failure recurred since the item was created?
If the failure has recurred: this is a repeat failure — the item must be closed immediately, not amnestied
If the failure has not recurred: assess whether the systemic condition that caused the incident still exists
If the systemic condition no longer exists (system was replaced, architecture changed): close as 'superseded'
If the systemic condition still exists but risk is tolerable: formal risk acceptance with a review date
Document the amnesty decision in the postmortem record — it is not silent deletion
Backlog amnesty is a reset, not a pattern. If a team runs amnesty more than once every 18–24 months, it is a signal that the reliability budget or the action item process is structurally broken.
Cross-Team Action Item Dependencies

Some postmortem action items require changes from more than one team — a monitoring improvement might need the platform team to add a new metric and the application team to wire it to their code. Cross-team items are the most likely to stall because no single team owns the whole item.
Cross-Team Item Rules
Each component of a cross-team item must be split into separate action items, one per team, with the same postmortem link
A designated coordinator (typically the IC or EM of the team that experienced the incident) is responsible for keeping the parallel items in sync
The item is not CLOSED until all component items are CLOSED — partial completion does not count
If the dependency is blocking, the component items link to each other as blockers
Cross-Team Escalation

If one team's component is blocking another team's work from closing, the coordinator escalates to the Engineering Managers of both teams. If both EMs cannot resolve the dependency within one week, it escalates to the Director of Engineering to prioritise and resource.
Scenario
Coordinator Action
Escalation Path
Team A's component is OPEN; Team B's is ready
Coordinator messages Team A's EM for sprint intake confirmation
Team A EM → Director if not in next sprint
Teams disagree on implementation approach
IC convenes a 30-min design sync
EM joint decision; Director if no agreement in 48h
External team (not Anthropic-owned) owns a component
Coordinator files external request and tracks externally
Security or Legal depending on vendor type
Quarterly Backlog Review
In addition to the weekly EM triage, the incident action backlog receives a quarterly review at the director level. The quarterly review surfaces patterns that are invisible at the team level.
Quarterly Review Agenda
Aggregate backlog health metrics across all teams: backlog size, mean item age, P1-overdue count, stale item count
Identify systems with disproportionate item accumulation — teams that are producing items faster than they close them
Review formal risk acceptance decisions made in the quarter: are the compensating controls still in place?
Cross-reference backlog items with recurring failure patterns from the postmortem library — are the same root cause tags appearing repeatedly across different teams?
Resource allocation review: are teams with the highest incident rate allocated the highest reliability budget?
Action: adjust reliability budgets for the next quarter based on findings
Quarterly Backlog Report Template
Summary

Incident action backlog governance ensures that postmortem action items are prioritised, resourced, and tracked to closure rather than accumulating as invisible reliability debt. The reliability capacity budget reserves 15–35% of sprint capacity for reliability work. Weekly triage uses a three-dimension priority score. Items older than 90 days require director review. Formal risk acceptance provides a documented path for items that cannot be closed within the normal SLA.
Reliability budget: 15–30% of sprint capacity depending on incident rate; P1-band items enter the sprint regardless of budget pressure
Priority score: recurrence risk (x3) + blast radius (x3) + ease (x1); P1-band at score >= 15
Age governance: 30-day triage, 60-day formal deferral, 90-day director review, no stale items tolerated
Formal risk acceptance requires EM sign-off, a compensating control, and a review date within 90 days
Action items live on the sprint board with the same visibility as feature work — not in a separate tool
Backlog amnesty is a one-time reset, not a recurring practice
